author: The Third Ecumenical Council: The Council of Ephesus
book: The XII. Anathematisms of St. Cyril Against Nestorius.

classification.cautionReason: This work discusses heresy in order to refute it; do not classify it as heretical merely from its title.

lineage: The Third Ecumenical Council: The Council of Ephesus.
The XII. Anathematisms of St. Cyril Against Nestorius.

metadata.author: The Third Ecumenical Council: The Council of Ephesus

title: Excursus on the Word Θεοτόκος.

verses.book: The XII. Anathematisms of St. Cyril Against Nestorius.
verses.text: There have been some who have tried to reduce all the great theological controversies on the Trinity and on the Incarnation to mere logomachies, and have jeered at those who could waste their time and energies over such trivialities.

verses.book: The XII. Anathematisms of St. Cyril Against Nestorius.
verses.text: For example, it has been said that the real difference between Arius and Athanasius was nothing more nor less than an iota, and that even Athanasius himself, in his more placid, and therefore presumably more rational moods, was willing to hold communion with those who differed from him and who still rejected the homousion.

verses.book: The XII. Anathematisms of St. Cyril Against Nestorius.
verses.text: But however catching and brilliant such remarks may be, they lack all solid foundation in truth.

verses.book: The XII. Anathematisms of St. Cyril Against Nestorius.
verses.text: It is perfectly manifest that a person so entirely lacking in discrimination as not to see the enormous difference between identity and likeness is not one whose opinion on such a point can be of much value.

verses.book: The XII. Anathematisms of St. Cyril Against Nestorius.
verses.text: A brilliant historian is not necessarily an accurate historian, far less need he be a safe guide in matters of theological definition.

verses.book: The XII. Anathematisms of St. Cyril Against Nestorius.
verses.text: A similar attempt to reduce to a logomachy the difference between the Catholic faith and Nestorianism has been made by some writers of undoubted learning among Protestants, notably by Fuchs and Schröckh.

verses.book: The XII. Anathematisms of St. Cyril Against Nestorius.
verses.text: But as in the case of the homousios so, too, in the case of the theotocos the word expresses a great, necessary, and fundamental doctrine of the Catholic faith.

verses.book: The XII. Anathematisms of St. Cyril Against Nestorius.
verses.text: It is not a matter of words, but of things, and the mind most unskilled in theology cannot fail to grasp the enormous difference there is between affirming, as does Nestorianism, that a God indwelt a man with a human personality of his own distinct from the personality of the indwelling god; and that God assumed to himself human nature, that is a human body and a human soul, but without human personality.

verses.book: The XII. Anathematisms of St. Cyril Against Nestorius.
verses.text: Bright, St. Leo on the Incarnation, pp.

verses.book: The XII. Anathematisms of St. Cyril Against Nestorius.
verses.text: It is, then, clear that the question raised by the wide circulation of the discourses of Nestorius as archbishop of Constantinople was not verbal, but vital.

verses.book: The XII. Anathematisms of St. Cyril Against Nestorius.
verses.text: Much of his language was irrelevant, and indicated some confusedness of thought: much would, of itself, admit of an orthodox construction; in one of the latest of his sermons, which Garnier dates on Sunday, December 14, 430, he grants that “Theotocos” might be used as signifying that “the temple which was formed in Mary by the Holy Spirit was united to the Godhead;” but it was impossible not to ask whether by “the temple” he meant the body of Jesus, or Jesus himself regarded as a human individual existing ἰδίᾳ, ἰδικῶς, ἀνὰ μέρος—as Cyril represents his theory—and whether by “union” he meant more than a close alliance, ejusdem generis, in the last analysis, with the relation between God and every saint, or, indeed, every Christian in true moral fellowship with him—an alliance which would amount, in Cyril’s phrase, to no more than a “relative union,” and would reduce the Saviour to a “Theophoros,” the title claimed of old by one of his chief martyrs.

verses.book: The XII. Anathematisms of St. Cyril Against Nestorius.
verses.text: And the real identity of Nestorius’s view with that of Theodore [of Mopsuestia] was but too plainly exhibited by such statements as occur in some of the extracts preserved in Cyril’s treatise Against Nestorius—to the effect that Christ was one with the Word by participation in dignity; that “the man” was partaker of Divine power, and in that sense not mere man; that he was adored together with the Word; and that “My Lord and my God” was a doxology to the Father; and above all, by the words spoken at Ephesus, “I can never allow that a child of three months old was God.”

verses.book: The XII. Anathematisms of St. Cyril Against Nestorius.
verses.text: It is no part of my duty to defend the truth of either the Catholic or Nestorian proposition—each has found many adherents in most ages since it was first started, and probably what is virtually Nestorianism is to-day far more widely held among persons deemed to be orthodox than is commonly supposed.

verses.book: The XII. Anathematisms of St. Cyril Against Nestorius.
verses.text: Be this as it may, Nestorianism is clearly subversive of the whole Catholic Doctrine of the Incarnation, and therefore the importance of the word Θεοτόκος cannot be exaggerated.

verses.book: The XII. Anathematisms of St. Cyril Against Nestorius.
verses.text: I shall treat the word Theotocos under two heads; (1) Its history (2) its meaning, first however quoting Bp.

verses.book: The XII. Anathematisms of St. Cyril Against Nestorius.
verses.text: Pearson’s words on its Conciliar authority.

verses.book: The XII. Anathematisms of St. Cyril Against Nestorius.
verses.text: (Pearson, Exp. of the Creed, Art.

verses.book: The XII. Anathematisms of St. Cyril Against Nestorius.
verses.text: “It is plain that the Council of Ephesus which condemned Nestorius confirmed this title Θεοτόκος; I say confirmed it; for it is evident that it was before used in the Church, by the tumult which arose at the first denial of it by Anastasius [Nestorius’s presbyter]; and so confirmed it as received before, because they approved the Epistles of St. Cyril, who proved it by the usage of those Fathers which preceded him.”

verses.book: The XII. Anathematisms of St. Cyril Against Nestorius.
verses.text: (1) History of Word Θεοτόκος.

verses.book: The XII. Anathematisms of St. Cyril Against Nestorius.
verses.text: It has not been unfrequently assumed that the word Theotocos was coined to express the peculiar view of the Incarnation held by St. Cyril.

verses.book: The XII. Anathematisms of St. Cyril Against Nestorius.
verses.text: Such however, is an entire mistake.

verses.book: The XII. Anathematisms of St. Cyril Against Nestorius.
verses.text: It was an old term of Catholic Theology, and the very word was used by bishop Alexander in a letter from the synod held at Alexandria in a.d.

verses.book: The XII. Anathematisms of St. Cyril Against Nestorius.
verses.text: 320, to condemn the Arian heresy (more than a hundred years before the meeting of the Council of Ephesus); “After this, we receive the doctrine of the resurrection from the dead, of which Jesus Christ our Lord became the first-fruits; who bore a body in truth, not in semblance, which he derived from Mary the Mother of God (ἐκ τῆς Θεοτόκου Μαρίας).”

verses.book: The XII. Anathematisms of St. Cyril Against Nestorius.
verses.text: The same word had been used by many church writers among whom may be mentioned St. Athanasius, who says, “As the flesh was born of Mary, the Mother of God, so we say that he, the Word, was himself born of Mary” (Orat. c.

verses.book: The XII. Anathematisms of St. Cyril Against Nestorius.
verses.text: Arian., iij., 14, 29, 33; also iv., 32).

verses.text: See also Eusebius (Vit.

verses.text: Const., iij., 43); St. Cyril of Jerusalem (Cat., x., 9); and especially Origen, who (says Bp.

verses.book: The XII. Anathematisms of St. Cyril Against Nestorius.
verses.text: Pearson) “did not only use, but expound at large the meaning of that title Θεοτόκος in his first tome on the Epistle to the Romans, as Socrates and Liberatus testify.”

verses.book: The XII. Anathematisms of St. Cyril Against Nestorius.
verses.text: Origen in Deut. xxii., 23; vol. ij., p.

verses.book: The XII. Anathematisms of St. Cyril Against Nestorius.
The XII. Anathematisms of St. Cyril Against Nestorius.
verses.text: In fact Theodore of Mopsuestia was the first to object to it, so far as we know, writing as follows: “Mary bare Jesus, not the Word, for the Word was and remained omnipresent, although from the beginning he dwelt in Jesus in a peculiar manner.

verses.book: The XII. Anathematisms of St. Cyril Against Nestorius.
verses.text: Thus Mary is properly the Mother of Christ (Christotocos) but not the mother of God (Theotocos).

verses.book: The XII. Anathematisms of St. Cyril Against Nestorius.
verses.text: Only figuratively, per anaphoram, can she be called Theotocos also, because God was in Christ in a remarkable manner.

verses.book: The XII. Anathematisms of St. Cyril Against Nestorius.
verses.text: Properly she bare a man, in whom the union with the Word was begun, but was still so little completed, that he was not yet called the Son of God.”

verses.book: The XII. Anathematisms of St. Cyril Against Nestorius.
verses.text: And in another place he says: “It is madness to say that God is born of the Virgin.…Not God, but the temple in which God dwelt, is born of Mary.”

verses.book: The XII. Anathematisms of St. Cyril Against Nestorius.
verses.text: How far Theodore had departed from the teaching of the Apostolic days may be seen by the following quotations from St. Ignatius.

verses.book: The XII. Anathematisms of St. Cyril Against Nestorius.
verses.text: “There is one only physician, of flesh and spirit, generate and ingenerate, God in man, true Life in death, Son of Mary and of God, first passible and then impassible, Jesus Christ our Lord.”

verses.book: The XII. Anathematisms of St. Cyril Against Nestorius.
verses.text: Further on in the same epistle he says: “For our God, Jesus the Christ, was borne in the womb by Mary etc.” With the first of these passages Bp.

verses.book: The XII. Anathematisms of St. Cyril Against Nestorius.
verses.text: Lightfoot very aptly compares the following from Melito.

verses.book: The XII. Anathematisms of St. Cyril Against Nestorius.
verses.text: “Since he was incorporeal, he fashioned a body for himself of our likeness…he was carried by Mary and clothed by his Father, he trod the earth and he filled the heavens.”

verses.book: The XII. Anathematisms of St. Cyril Against Nestorius.
verses.text: Theodore was forced by the exigencies of his position to deny the doctrine of the communicatio idiomatum which had already at that early date come to be well understood, at least so far as practice is concerned.

verses.book: The XII. Anathematisms of St. Cyril Against Nestorius.
verses.text: (Hefele, Hist. of the Councils, Vol. iii., p.

verses.book: The XII. Anathematisms of St. Cyril Against Nestorius.
verses.text: This doctrine, as is well known is predicating the same properties of the two natures in Christ, not in abstracto (Godhead and manhood), but in concreto (God and man).

verses.book: The XII. Anathematisms of St. Cyril Against Nestorius.
verses.text: Christ himself had declared in St. John iii., 16: “God…gave his only begotten Son” (namely, to death), and similarly St. Peter declared (Acts iii., 15): “ye…killed the Prince of Life,” when in fact the being given up and being killed is a property (ἰδίωμα = predicate) of man, not of God (the only begotten, the Prince of Life).

verses.book: The XII. Anathematisms of St. Cyril Against Nestorius.
verses.text: In the same way Clement of Rome, for example, spoke of “the sufferings of God” (παθήματα Θεοῦ) (1 Ad Cor.

verses.book: The XII. Anathematisms of St. Cyril Against Nestorius.
verses.text: 2), Ignatius of Antioch (Ad Ephes., c.

verses.book: The XII. Anathematisms of St. Cyril Against Nestorius.
verses.text: 1, and Ad Rom., 6) of an αἷμα and πάθος Θεοῦ, Tatian of a Θεὸς πεπονθὼς (Ad Græcos, c.

verses.book: The XII. Anathematisms of St. Cyril Against Nestorius.
verses.text: 13); Barnabas teaches (c.

verses.book: The XII. Anathematisms of St. Cyril Against Nestorius.
verses.text: 7) that “the Son of God could not suffer except on our behalf…and on our behalf he has brought the vessel of his Spirit as a sacrifice.”

verses.book: The XII. Anathematisms of St. Cyril Against Nestorius.
verses.text: Similarly Irenæus (iii., 16, 6) says, “The Only-begotten impassible Word (unigenitus impassibilis) has become passible” (passibilis); and Athanasius, ἐσταυρώμενον εἶναι Θεὸν (Ep. ad Epictet., n.

verses.book: The XII. Anathematisms of St. Cyril Against Nestorius.
The XII. Anathematisms of St. Cyril Against Nestorius.
verses.text: It is, however, to be remarked that the properties of the one nature were never transferred to the other nature in itself, but always to the Person who is at the same time both man and God.

verses.book: The XII. Anathematisms of St. Cyril Against Nestorius.
verses.text: Human attributes were not ascribed to the Godhead, but to God, and vice versâ.

verses.book: The XII. Anathematisms of St. Cyril Against Nestorius.
verses.text: For a full treatment of the figure of speech called the communicatio idiomatum the reader is referred to the great works on Theology where it will be found set forth at large, with its restrictions specified and with examples of its use.

verses.book: The XII. Anathematisms of St. Cyril Against Nestorius.
verses.text: A brief but interesting note on it will be found in St. John Damascene’s famous treatise De Fide Orthodoxa, Book III., iij.

verses.book: The XII. Anathematisms of St. Cyril Against Nestorius.
verses.text: (Migne’s Pat.

verses.book: The XII. Anathematisms of St. Cyril Against Nestorius.
verses.text: (2) Meaning of the Word Θεοτόκος.

verses.book: The XII. Anathematisms of St. Cyril Against Nestorius.
verses.text: We pass now to the meaning of the word, having sufficiently traced the history of its use.

verses.book: The XII. Anathematisms of St. Cyril Against Nestorius.
verses.text: Bishop Pearson says: “This name was first in use in the Greek Church, who, delighting in the happy compositions of that language, called the blessed Virgin Theotocos.

verses.book: The XII. Anathematisms of St. Cyril Against Nestorius.
verses.text: From whence the Latins in imitation styled her Virginem Deiparam et Deigenitricem.”

verses.book: The XII. Anathematisms of St. Cyril Against Nestorius.
verses.text: In the passage to which the words just quoted are a portion of a footnote, he says: “Wherefore from these three, a true conception, nutrition, and parturition, we must acknowledge that the blessed Virgin was truly and properly the Mother of our Saviour.

verses.book: The XII. Anathematisms of St. Cyril Against Nestorius.
verses.text: And so is she frequently styled the Mother of Jesus in the language of the Evangelists, and by Elizabeth particularly the ‘Mother of her Lord,’ as also by the general consent of the Church (because he which was so born of her was God,) the Deipara; which being a compound title begun in the Greek Church, was resolved into its parts by the Latins and so the Virgin was plainly named the Mother of God.”

verses.book: The XII. Anathematisms of St. Cyril Against Nestorius.
verses.text: Pearson is mistaken in supposing that the resolution of the compound Theotocos into μήτηρ τοῦ Θεοῦ was unknown to the early Greek writers.

verses.book: The XII. Anathematisms of St. Cyril Against Nestorius.
verses.text: Dionysius expressly calls Mary ἡ μήτηρ τοῦ Θεοῦ μου (Contr.

verses.book: The XII. Anathematisms of St. Cyril Against Nestorius.
verses.text: Samos., Quæst. viij.); and among the Latins Mater Dei and Dei Genetrix were (as Pearson himself confesses in note 37) used before the time of St. Leo I.

verses.book: The XII. Anathematisms of St. Cyril Against Nestorius.
verses.text: It is not an open question whether Mater Dei, Dei Genetrix, Deipara, μήτηρ τοῦ Θεοῦ are proper equivalents for Θεοτόκος.

verses.book: The XII. Anathematisms of St. Cyril Against Nestorius.
verses.text: This point has been settled by the unvarying use of the whole Church of God throughout all the ages from that day to this, but there is, or at least some persons have thought that there was, some question as to how Theotocos should be translated into English.

verses.book: The XII. Anathematisms of St. Cyril Against Nestorius.
verses.text: Throughout this volume I have translated it “Mother of God,” and I propose giving my reasons for considering this the only accurate translation of the word, both from a lexico-graphical and from a theological point of view.

verses.book: The XII. Anathematisms of St. Cyril Against Nestorius.
verses.text: (a) It is evident that the word is a composite formed of Θεός = God, and τίκτειν = to be the mother of a child.

verses.book: The XII. Anathematisms of St. Cyril Against Nestorius.
verses.text: Now I have translated the verbal part “to be the mother of a child” because “to bear” in English does not necessarily carry the full meaning of the Greek word, which (as Bp.

verses.book: The XII. Anathematisms of St. Cyril Against Nestorius.
verses.text: Pearson has well remarked in the passage cited above) includes “conception, nutrition, and parturition.”

verses.book: The XII. Anathematisms of St. Cyril Against Nestorius.
verses.text: It has been suggested that “God-bearer” is an exact translation.

verses.book: The XII. Anathematisms of St. Cyril Against Nestorius.
verses.text: To this I object, that in the first place it is not English; and in the second that it would be an equally and, to my mind, more accurate translation of Θεοφόρος than of Θεοτόκος.

verses.book: The XII. Anathematisms of St. Cyril Against Nestorius.
verses.text: Another suggestion is that it be rendered “the bringer forth of God.”

verses.book: The XII. Anathematisms of St. Cyril Against Nestorius.
verses.text: Again I object that, from a rhetorical standpoint, the expression is very open to criticism; and from a lexicographical point of view it is entirely inadequate, for while indeed the parturition does necessarily involve in the course of nature the previous conception and nutrition, it certainly does not express it.

verses.book: The XII. Anathematisms of St. Cyril Against Nestorius.
verses.text: Now the word Mother does necessarily express all three of these when used in relation to her child.

verses.book: The XII. Anathematisms of St. Cyril Against Nestorius.
verses.text: The reader will remember that the question I am discussing is not whether Mary can properly be called the Mother of God; this Nestorius denied and many in ancient and modern times have been found to agree with him.

verses.book: The XII. Anathematisms of St. Cyril Against Nestorius.
verses.text: The question I am considering is what the Greek word Theotocos means in English.

verses.book: The XII. Anathematisms of St. Cyril Against Nestorius.
verses.text: I do not think anyone would hesitate to translate Nestorius’s Christotocos by “Mother of Christ” and surely the expressions are identical from a lexicographical point of view.

verses.book: The XII. Anathematisms of St. Cyril Against Nestorius.
verses.text: Liddell and Scott in their Lexicon insert the word θεοτόκος as an adjective and translate “bearing God” and add: “especially ἡ Θεοτόκος, Mother of God, of the Virgin, Eccl.”

verses.book: The XII. Anathematisms of St. Cyril Against Nestorius.
verses.text: (b) It only remains to consider whether there is from a theological point of view any objection to the translation, “Mother of God.”

verses.book: The XII. Anathematisms of St. Cyril Against Nestorius.
verses.text: It is true that some persons have thought that such a rendering implied that the Godhead has its origin in Mary, but this was the very objection which Nestorius and his followers urged against the word Theotocos, and this being the case, it constitutes a strong argument in favour of the accuracy of the rendering.

verses.book: The XII. Anathematisms of St. Cyril Against Nestorius.
verses.text: Of course the answer to the objection in each case is the same, it is not of the Godhead that Mary is the Mother, but of the Incarnate Son, who is God.

verses.book: The XII. Anathematisms of St. Cyril Against Nestorius.
verses.text: “Mother” expresses exactly the relation to the incarnate Son which St. Cyril, the Council of Ephesus, and all succeeding, not to say also preceding, ages of Catholics, rightly or wrongly, ascribe to Mary.

verses.book: The XII. Anathematisms of St. Cyril Against Nestorius.
verses.text: All that every child derives from its Mother that God the Son derived from Mary, and this without the co-operation of any man, but by the direct operation of the Holy Ghost, so that in a fuller, truer, and more perfect sense, Mary is the Mother of God the Son in his incarnation, than any other earthly mother is of her son.

verses.book: The XII. Anathematisms of St. Cyril Against Nestorius.
verses.text: I therefore consider it certain that no scholar who can and will divest himself of theological bias, can doubt that “Mother of God” is the most accurate translation of the term Theotocos.
